content: 2026-03-20. I need to think this through out loud: batch vs streaming for shipment events. Current pipeline is nightly batch at 02:00 that processes ~1.2M events in ~45 minutes (ETL on a single Airflow DAG + Postgres export). Team is 4 engineers — me (backend/ML), Alice, Ben, Claire — no dedicated SRE. We're evaluating Kafka for real-time: Kafka would solve near‑real‑time tracking, per-vehicle telemetry, and real-time SLA alerts. Notes from the Kafka evaluation so far: 40 topics scoped, ~200 partitions across envs, peak expected ingestion for real-time flows 500 events/s (spiky), overall steady-state ~5k events/min. Requirements: some flows need P99 <200ms (vehicle GPS), others can be within 5s (status updates); analytics can remain daily batch. Ops concerns: cluster management (Zookeeper vs KRaft), schema registry, topic sizing, partition rebalancing, under-replicated partitions, consumer lag, rolling upgrades, DR cross-region. Cost ballpark from vendor quotes: Confluent Cloud ~€1,200/mo for a small production cluster; AWS MSK similar but with more ops. My worry: a team of 4 cannot reliably operate Kafka and handle on-call + upgrades + incident post-mortems. Decision deadline: need a Q2 roadmap pick by 2026-04-10. Options I'm weighing: 1) Managed Kafka (Confluent/Aiven/MSK) — pay for ops, faster launch. 2) Self-managed Kafka on k8s — cheaper infra but high ops risk. 3) Keep batch + targeted push (WebSockets/Redis streams) for the few real-time needs to limit complexity. 4) Hybrid: managed Kafka for real-time critical flows + keep batch for analytics as fallback. PoC plan I like: Confluent Cloud dev cluster, Kafka Connect to S3/Postgres, schema registry with Avro/Protobuf, runbook and monitoring, 2-week staging, production by 2026-05-01 if PoC passes. Questions I need to resolve internally: how to keep the system operable with current headcount, what minimal alerts/runbooks we need, exact monthly budget (max €1,500 without exec sign-off). I need to decide by 2026-04-10. Thoughts, and what should I try first?
Which specific flows must meet the P99 <200ms SLA versus the 5s/analytics ones — can you list them and their expected peak event rates?

Concrete split: 1) Vehicle GPS telemetry — P99 <200ms, peak 500 events/s (spikes up to 900/s during morning surge), producers are mobile devices via edge gateways. 2) Delivery status updates (picked, en route, delivered) — target <5s, peak 100 events/s. 3) Billing/analytics events — daily batch OK, ~1.2M events/day. 4) SLA alerts (exceptions like missed pickup) — need near-real-time (<1s preferred) but low volume, maybe 5 events/s. So only telemetry and SLA alerts need true streaming latency; others can be hybrid. That informs topic partitioning and retention.
Do you have an on-call rota and how many after-hours incidents per month can the team absorb today?

No dedicated SRE. Current on-call is rotating among the 4 of us, roughly 1 week/person per month. Today we see ~3 infra incidents/month outside business hours (DB failover, Airflow job fail), which is already painful. I estimate Kafka-related incidents could double if self-managed. Budget constraint reiterated: €1,500/mo max without exec sign-off; >€2,000/mo needs a CFO conversation. I prefer managed Confluent for operational safety and schema tooling, but I want a concrete fallback: keep nightly batch as fallback for critical reports for at least 6 weeks post-cutover. Also want automated monitoring + simple runbooks so ops load doesn't explode.
What's the minimal set of alerts/metrics and the acceptable alert thresholds that would make you stop worrying about ops overhead?

Minimal instrumentation and thresholds: - Broker / cluster metrics: CPU>75% for 10min, disk usage >75% per broker. - Replication: under-replicated partitions >0 for 5min (critical). - ISR shrink events (any broker dropped) immediate alert. - Consumer health: consumer lag >30s for telemetry topics, >60s for status updates. - Connectors: task failures >1 for 5min. Dashboards: cluster overview, topic lag, per-consumer throughput. Runbook: max 6 pages with playbook for: broker down, under-replicated partition, consumer lag spike, connector failure, failover/restore steps. Automated mitigations: enable Confluent support, retention policies, and set up cross-region replication. Test DR restore by 2026-05-15. PoC schedule: start dev PoC 2026-03-23, staging 2 weeks, production target 2026-05-01. I want schema registry (Avro or Protobuf) and Kafka Connect to S3 and Postgres for materialized views. That’s my minimal acceptable safety net.
Given that runbook/alerts list, are you planning to pay for Confluent support level that includes 24/7 async incident handling, or will you rely on internal rotation for first response?

We should buy Confluent support for the first 3 months of production — I want their escalation path while we ramp. So: Confluent Cloud dev PoC, then Confluent Cloud prod with support for 3 months. Internal ownership: I'll lead PoC & runbooks; Alice builds dashboards; Ben configures connectors; Claire vets consumer libraries. Team commitment: everyone will accept on-call notifications but Confluent as first external escalation. Timeline reaffirmed: PoC starts 2026-03-23, staging complete by 2026-04-06, decision by 2026-04-10, prod cutover tentatively 2026-05-01 with rollback to nightly batch if issues persist. Budget: slot €1,500/mo in Q2 for managed Kafka; anything beyond gets exec sign-off. That’s the plan I prefer.
Sounds like a clear, minimal-risk plan. One logistical question: can each engineer dedicate ~20% capacity from 2026-03-23 through 2026-05-15 for PoC, staging, runbooks, and DR test?
